questions.questionText: USG of the abdomen in a trauma patient (FAST exam) evaluates for free fluid in four areas. Which is NOT a standard FAST window?
questions.optionB: Retroperitoneal space (NOT routinely evaluated in standard FAST — FAST looks at intraperitoneal and pericardial spaces)
questions.explanation: Core concept: FAST = Focused Assessment with Sonography in Trauma — 4 windows. Why correct: Standard FAST: (1) Subxiphoid (pericardial effusion/tamponade), (2) RUQ — Morrison's pouch (hepatorenal), (3) LUQ — splenorenal recess, (4) Suprapubic/pelvic (free fluid in pelvis). Retroperitoneum NOT evaluated (needs CT). Why A wrong: Morrison's pouch = most sensitive for intraperitoneal free fluid (most dependent in supine). Why C wrong: Splenorenal recess — standard LUQ window. Why D wrong: Pelvic is the 4th standard window. Clinical relevance: eFAST adds bilateral pleural spaces (pneumothorax/hemothorax). FAST positive in unstable patient → operating room. Exam trap: FAST is SCREENING only — negative FAST does NOT exclude injury (sensitivity ~85%). CT is definitive. Memory aid: 'FAST = Four Areas: Subxiphoid, hepatoRenal, splenoRenal, Pelvis.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Ultrasound.

questions.questionText: On obstetric USG, nuchal translucency (NT) is measured between which weeks of gestation?
questions.optionB: 11-13+6 weeks (11 weeks to 13 weeks 6 days — increased NT ≥3.5mm associated with Down syndrome and other aneuploidies)
questions.explanation: Core concept: NT measurement is a first trimester screening test for chromosomal abnormalities. Why correct: NT = fluid collection behind fetal neck on sagittal section, measured at 11-13+6 weeks (CRL 45-84mm). NT ≥3.5mm: increased risk of trisomy 21, 18, 13, Turner syndrome, cardiac defects. Combined with PAPP-A + free β-hCG = combined first trimester screening. Why A wrong: Too early — fetus too small for NT measurement. Why C wrong: 16-20 weeks = anomaly scan/quadruple test timing. Why D wrong: 24-28 = growth assessment. Clinical relevance: If NT increased → invasive testing (CVS 11-13 weeks or amniocentesis 15-18 weeks). NIPT (cell-free fetal DNA) increasingly used as alternative. Exam trap: NT screening window is STRICT (11-13+6 weeks). Outside this window, measurement is unreliable. Memory aid: 'NT = Nuchal at 11-13 weeks = Chromosomal screening.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Ultrasound.

questions.questionText: Target sign (doughnut sign) on transverse USG of the abdomen in a child with intermittent colicky pain and bloody stools is diagnostic of:
questions.optionB: Intussusception (telescoping bowel within bowel — target appearance on transverse, pseudokidney on longitudinal)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Target/doughnut sign on USG = intussusception. Why correct: Intussusception — proximal bowel telescopes into distal → concentric rings of bowel wall on cross-section (target/doughnut). Longitudinal: pseudokidney sign. Peak age 6-9 months. Ileocolic most common. Why A wrong: Appendicitis — non-compressible tubular structure >6mm, no target sign. Why C wrong: Pyloric stenosis — elongated pyloric channel >16mm, muscle thickness >3mm, 'donut sign' of pylorus (different structure). Why D wrong: Malrotation — abnormal SMA/SMV relationship (SMV to left of SMA). Clinical relevance: Air/saline reduction enema under USG guidance = treatment (non-operative). If failed or peritonitis → surgery. Exam trap: Both intussusception and pyloric stenosis can show circular appearances — but structures are different (bowel vs pylorus). Memory aid: 'Target = Telescoped bowel = InTussusception.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Ultrasound.

questions.questionText: USG of the thyroid shows a hypoechoic nodule with microcalcifications, irregular margins, and taller-than-wide shape. The TIRADS score suggests:
questions.optionB: High suspicion for malignancy (TIRADS 5 — microcalcifications + hypoechoic + irregular margins = highly suspicious, FNAC recommended)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Microcalcifications + hypoechoic + irregular margins = high suspicion for thyroid malignancy (papillary carcinoma). Why correct: ACR TIRADS scoring: composition (solid=2), echogenicity (hypoechoic=2), shape (taller-than-wide=3), margin (irregular=2), echogenic foci (microcalcifications=3). ≥7 points = TIRADS 5 = highly suspicious. Why A wrong: Benign features include purely cystic, comet-tail artifacts, spongiform. Why C wrong: Simple cysts score TIRADS 1. Why D wrong: TIRADS precisely categorizes nodules. Clinical relevance: Microcalcifications (psammoma bodies) are most specific for papillary thyroid carcinoma. FNAC indicated for TIRADS 4-5. Exam trap: Macrocalcifications are less specific than microcalcifications. Comet-tail artifact = benign (colloid). Memory aid: 'MICROcalcifications = Malignancy Indicator in thyroid.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Ultrasound.

questions.questionText: In a pregnant woman, the most reliable USG parameter for dating in the first trimester is:
questions.optionB: Crown-rump length (CRL — most accurate dating parameter in first trimester, accurate to ±5 days between 7-13 weeks)
questions.explanation: Core concept: CRL is the most accurate first-trimester dating parameter. Why correct: CRL measured at 7-13 weeks, accurate to ±3-5 days. After first trimester, BPD becomes most commonly used (accurate to ±1-2 weeks). Why A wrong: BPD — best in second trimester (14-20 weeks). Why C wrong: Femur length — complementary measurement, less accurate for dating alone. Why D wrong: Abdominal circumference — for growth assessment (third trimester), not dating. Clinical relevance: If discrepancy between LMP dates and USG dating >7 days in first trimester, USG dates are used. Exam trap: CRL accuracy DECREASES after 13 weeks (biological variation increases). First trimester = most accurate dating period. Memory aid: 'CRL = Crown-to-Rump = 1st trimester dating gold standard.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Ultrasound.
